tests.title: IELTS Academic Reading — Test 07
tests.description: Original full-length Academic Reading test: 3 passages, 40 questions, 60 minutes.

sections.title: Passage 1 — Borrowing Instead of Buying
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 1–13.
sections.passage:
Borrowing Instead of Buying

¶1 In a typical household, a great many possessions spend almost all their lives doing nothing. The electric drill is the classic example: the average one is used, over its entire lifetime, for only a few minutes, yet nearly every home contains one. The same is true of ladders, tents, sewing machines and party crockery, expensive to buy, awkward to store, and needed only on rare occasions. A growing movement argues that owning such things outright, one per household, is a remarkably wasteful way to meet an occasional need. Multiply that waste across a whole street, where every house owns its own idle drill and ladder, and the scale of the redundancy becomes hard to defend.

¶2 The alternative is an old idea in a new form: the library of things. Modelled on the ordinary book library, it lends not novels but objects, allowing members to borrow a drill, a pressure washer or a fondue set for a few days and return it when the job is done. The principle is exactly that which underpins the public library, namely that an item used intensively by many people in turn need not be owned by any of them. What libraries have long done for knowledge, these new institutions propose to do for the contents of the garden shed. The shift in thinking is small but profound: from a world organised around having to one organised around access.

¶3 The appeal is partly financial. A household that borrows the few expensive tools it rarely needs can avoid spending a considerable sum on objects destined to gather dust. For people on low incomes, in particular, a library of things can put within reach equipment they could never justify buying, from carpet cleaners to camping gear. The saving is not only of money but of space, a serious consideration in the small flats of crowded cities, where a cupboard surrendered to a once-a-year appliance is a real sacrifice. For many city dwellers, space has become quite as precious as money.

¶4 The environmental case is, if anything, stronger. Every drill that is shared is a drill that need not be manufactured, packaged and shipped, with all the energy and raw materials that process consumes. If ten neighbours share a single ladder rather than buying ten, the saving in resources is plain. Supporters point out that a great deal of the environmental harm done by consumer goods occurs not in their use but in their making, so that simply producing fewer of them, by sharing those we have, can be among the most effective things a community can do. A single shared object, on this logic, quietly does the work of many. The greenest product, in this sense, is often the one that is never made at all.

¶5 Such schemes also turn out to do something their founders rarely anticipated: they build community. Borrowing a tool brings a person into a shared space, where staff and volunteers offer advice and where neighbours meet who might otherwise never have spoken. Some libraries of things have added workshops, teaching members how to use what they borrow or how to mend what they already own. In this way the institution becomes not merely a store of objects but a place of contact, recreating on a small scale the neighbourly lending that was once ordinary between people who knew one another. In an age of anonymous high streets, that rediscovery of the neighbourly can matter quite as much as the saving itself.

¶6 The obstacles, however, are real. Tools wear out, break or are returned damaged, and maintaining a usable collection demands money and effort that membership fees alone rarely cover. Many such projects survive only with grants or the unpaid labour of enthusiasts, and their finances are often precarious. There is also the simple matter of habit: most people are thoroughly accustomed to owning, and persuading them to borrow instead, to plan ahead and to accept that the drill they want may already be out, requires a genuine shift in expectations. Convenience, after all, has long been the great selling point of ownership, and borrowing asks people to trade a little of it for other goods.

¶7 Whether the library of things becomes a lasting feature of city life or remains a worthy experiment is not yet clear. What it represents, however, is a quiet challenge to an assumption that has gone largely unquestioned for a century: that the natural way to obtain what one needs is to buy and keep it. By proposing that access, rather than ownership, might often serve us better, these modest institutions raise a question with implications far beyond the garden shed. Perhaps we own a great many things, they suggest, that we would be better off simply being able to borrow. It is a small idea, but one with a surprisingly long reach into how we choose to live.

questions.text: The average electric drill is used for only a short time over its whole life.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says the average drill is used 'for only a few minutes' over its entire lifetime.

evidence.quote: for only a few minutes

questions.text: A library of things lends out books as well as objects.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says it 'lends not novels but objects', so it does not lend books.

evidence.quote: it lends not novels but objects

questions.text: Libraries of things can be especially useful for people on low incomes.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says that 'For people on low incomes, in particular', such a library puts otherwise unaffordable equipment within reach.

evidence.quote: For people on low incomes, in particular

questions.text: Most environmental harm from consumer goods happens while they are being used.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 says much harm occurs 'not in their use but in their making', contradicting the statement.

evidence.quote: not in their use but in their making

questions.text: Libraries of things are now more popular than public book libraries.
questions.explanation: The passage compares the two in principle but never compares their popularity, so this is Not Given.

questions.text: The new institution that lends objects is called a library of ______.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 names it 'the library of things'.

evidence.quote: the library of things

questions.text: Besides money, borrowing rather than buying also saves ______ in small flats.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says the saving is 'not only of money but of space', important in small flats.

evidence.quote: The saving is not only of money but of space

questions.text: Some libraries of things run ______ that teach members to use or mend items.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says some have 'added workshops, teaching members how to use what they borrow or how to mend what they already own'.

evidence.quote: added workshops, teaching members how to use what they borrow

questions.text: Which paragraph explains the financial savings for households and the poor?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 covers the financial appeal, including for those on low incomes, and saved space.

evidence.quote: The appeal is partly financial

questions.text: Which paragraph describes how these schemes unexpectedly bring people together?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says such schemes do something founders rarely anticipated: 'they build community'.

evidence.quote: they build community

questions.text: Which paragraph sets out the difficulties such projects face?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 describes the obstacles: tools wearing out, precarious finances and the habit of owning.

evidence.quote: The obstacles, however, are real

questions.text: Which paragraph argues these institutions challenge the assumption that we should buy and keep things?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 says they pose 'a quiet challenge to an assumption' that the natural way to get things is to buy and keep them.

evidence.quote: a quiet challenge to an assumption that has gone largely unquestioned

questions.text: According to Paragraph 7, the schemes suggest that ______, rather than ownership, might serve people better.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 proposes 'that access, rather than ownership, might often serve us better'.

evidence.quote: access, rather than ownership, might often serve us better

sections.title: Passage 2 — Letting the River Run
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 14–26.
sections.passage:
Letting the River Run

¶1 For more than a century, the dominant approach to rivers was to control them. Engineers straightened meandering channels to speed the flow, raised embankments to keep water off the land, and lined banks with concrete to stop them shifting. The aim was always the same: to make the river behave predictably, to protect the towns and farms that had grown up along it, and to claim for human use the flat, fertile land of the floodplain. For a long time this strategy appeared to work, and the engineered river became a symbol of a society's command over nature. To tame a river was, for generations of engineers, almost the definition of progress itself, and the straightened channel and the high embankment stood as monuments to that ambition.

¶2 In recent decades, however, the limitations of that approach have become painfully clear. A straightened, embanked river moves water downstream faster than a natural one, which means that when heavy rain falls across a whole catchment, the flood arrives lower down more quickly and more violently than it once did. Defences built to hold back a flood of a certain size are overwhelmed by a larger one, and the very confidence they inspire encourages yet more building on land that was never truly safe. Each higher wall, it turned out, often simply moved the problem somewhere else. Protecting one town more securely could, in effect, condemn the next one downstream to a worse flood than before. The water, in the end, has to go somewhere.

¶3 Out of this disappointment grew a different philosophy, one that works with a river rather than against it. Instead of forcing all the water downstream as fast as possible, this approach seeks to slow it down and store it temporarily where it can do least harm. Restoring a river's natural bends lengthens its course and checks its speed; reconnecting it to its floodplain gives the water somewhere to spread harmlessly when levels rise. The thinking represents a near-reversal of the old engineering instinct: the floodplain is treated not as land to be defended from the river but as part of the river itself. A river, in this understanding, is not an enemy to be walled out but a force to be accommodated. The task is to give it room, not to deny that it needs any.

¶4 One of the more striking discoveries has been the value of slowing water high up in a catchment, long before it reaches any town. Planting woodland on hillsides, allowing fallen trees to lie across small streams, and restoring the boggy uplands where rivers begin all help to hold rainwater back and release it gradually. The beaver, once hunted to local extinction in many regions, has proved an unexpectedly effective ally, since its dams pond water and smooth out the peaks of flow. Measures spread across a whole landscape, each modest in itself, can together blunt the surge that produces a dangerous flood. The principle is to treat the whole catchment, not just the riverbank, as the proper unit of management, for even a fallen log left where it lies can become a small and useful brake on the water.

¶5 Restored rivers bring benefits well beyond flood protection. A meandering, varied channel with marshes along its edges supports far more wildlife than a straight concrete ditch, and the wetlands that form filter pollutants from the water, improving its quality downstream. Such places store carbon, buffer droughts by holding moisture in the landscape, and offer people green space for recreation. A single project, justified at first purely as flood defence, often turns out to deliver a whole range of advantages that no concrete wall could ever provide. The same restored marsh that holds back a flood may also clean a town's water and shelter its birds. Few stretches of bare concrete can claim as much.

¶6 The approach is not without its difficulties. Giving a river room to flood means accepting that certain land, often farmland, will be deliberately allowed to flood, and persuading the people who own or use that land is rarely simple. Such schemes may require many landowners across a catchment to cooperate, and the benefits frequently fall to communities far downstream rather than to those who give up their fields. Working out how to share the costs fairly, so that those who bear the burden are not simply sacrificed for the good of strangers, has become one of the central challenges of the whole endeavour. A scheme that is technically sound but socially unjust, planners have learned, will not long survive contact with the people it affects. Fairness, here, is not a luxury but a condition of lasting success.

¶7 None of this means that hard defences have no place; a city cannot relocate, and walls will still be needed to protect dense, valuable areas. What has changed is the recognition that concrete alone is not enough, and that a river is a living system that responds, often unpredictably, to everything done to it. The most promising strategies now combine engineering with restoration, hard defences where they are unavoidable and natural processes wherever they can be allowed to work. After a century spent trying to conquer rivers, we are slowly learning the subtler art of living alongside them. It is, in a sense, a more modest ambition than conquest, and very probably a more durable public one.

options: Working with the river, not against it
Slowing the water at the source
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 describes the long-dominant aim 'to control them' through straightening and embanking.

evidence.quote: the dominant approach to rivers was to control them

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 2
options: Working with the river, not against it
Slowing the water at the source
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains how straightened, embanked rivers worsen floods downstream and that each wall 'simply moved the problem somewhere else'.

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 3
options: Working with the river, not against it
Slowing the water at the source
correct_answer: Working with the river, not against it
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 introduces a philosophy 'that works with a river rather than against it', slowing and storing water.

evidence.quote: works with a river rather than against it

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 4
options: Working with the river, not against it
Slowing the water at the source
correct_answer: Slowing the water at the source
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 covers 'slowing water high up in a catchment, long before it reaches any town'.

evidence.quote: slowing water high up in a catchment, long before it reaches any town

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 5
options: Working with the river, not against it
Slowing the water at the source
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says restored rivers 'bring benefits well beyond flood protection', from wildlife to carbon.

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 6
options: Working with the river, not against it
Slowing the water at the source
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 focuses on fairly sharing costs, since the burden and the benefits fall on different people.

questions.text: The traditional engineering approach aimed above all to make a river
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says the aim was 'to make the river behave predictably'.

evidence.quote: to make the river behave predictably

questions.text: A straightened, embanked river tends to make downstream floods
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says the flood 'arrives lower down more quickly and more violently than it once did'.

evidence.quote: more quickly and more violently than it once did

questions.text: In the newer approach, the floodplain is regarded as
options: land to be defended from the river
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says the floodplain is treated 'not as land to be defended from the river but as part of the river itself'.

evidence.quote: as part of the river itself

questions.text: Which animal is described as an unexpectedly effective ally in slowing water?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 says 'The beaver ... has proved an unexpectedly effective ally', since its dams pond water.

evidence.quote: has proved an unexpectedly effective ally

questions.text: Wetlands that form along restored rivers help to ______ pollutants from the water.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says the wetlands 'filter pollutants from the water, improving its quality downstream'.

evidence.quote: filter pollutants from the water

questions.text: Giving a river room to flood means accepting that certain ______ will be deliberately flooded.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 says it means accepting that 'certain land, often farmland, will be deliberately allowed to flood'.

evidence.quote: certain land, often farmland, will be deliberately allowed to flood

questions.text: Which TWO points does the final paragraph make about hard defences?
options: walls are still needed to protect dense, valuable areas
concrete alone is enough on its own
the best strategies combine engineering with restoration
all existing walls should be removed
correct_answer: walls are still needed to protect dense, valuable areas
the best strategies combine engineering with restoration
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 says 'walls will still be needed to protect dense, valuable areas' and that the best strategies 'combine engineering with restoration'.

evidence.quote: walls will still be needed to protect dense, valuable areas
combine engineering with restoration

sections.title: Passage 3 — Where Good Ideas Come From
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 27–40.
sections.passage:
Where Good Ideas Come From

¶1 We like to picture the great idea as a sudden flash, striking a lone genius in a single illuminated instant. The popular imagination is full of such moments: the apple falling, the cry of discovery in the bath. Yet historians and psychologists who have studied creativity closely tell a far less dramatic story. Important ideas, they find, almost never arrive complete in a single stroke. They are assembled slowly, often over years, out of fragments that drift through a mind until, eventually, they connect. The flash of insight, where it occurs at all, is usually the final click of a process that was long and largely invisible. What looks like a bolt from the blue is usually the last step of a journey nobody saw being taken, and the romance of the instant discovery survives chiefly because the long, dull middle of the story is so easily forgotten.

¶2 One reason ideas take so long is that most are combinations of older ones. A novelty rarely springs from nothing; far more often it is the unexpected joining of two things that already existed but had never been brought together. The printing press combined the wine press with movable type; countless inventions marry a technique from one field to a problem in another. It follows that the more varied the materials a mind has absorbed, the more combinations become possible, which is why broad curiosity, and exposure to fields beyond one's own, so often precedes original work. The specialist who reads only within their own field, by contrast, has fewer raw materials from which a genuine surprise might be made. Originality, in this light, tends to reward the magpie over the purist.

¶3 This helps to explain a striking historical pattern: creativity clusters. Certain cities and certain moments, a particular café, a single laboratory, a brief golden age, produce a wildly disproportionate share of the new. The cause is rarely a sudden concentration of born geniuses. It is that these were places where people from different backgrounds met and talked, where ideas could collide and recombine, and where the half-formed thought of one person could spark the imagination of another. Creativity, on this view, is less a property of individuals than of the connections between them. Move a person from such a place to an isolated one, and their output of new ideas often quietly falls.

¶4 If that is so, then the popular advice to simply think harder is largely misplaced. Professor Adaeze Mbeki argues that the conditions surrounding a person matter at least as much as the talent within them, and that an organisation serious about innovation should worry less about hiring exceptional individuals and more about how those individuals are allowed to interact. A brilliant mind kept in isolation, on her account, is a resource half wasted; the same mind placed where ideas flow freely will achieve far more. Environment, she insists, is not the backdrop to creativity but one of its main ingredients. Design the room well, she suggests, and the ideas will largely look after themselves. The hard part, on her account, is no longer the thinking but the arranging.

¶5 There is a further, more uncomfortable, requirement. Almost every genuinely new idea is, at first, indistinguishable from a bad one, since both depart from what is already accepted. A culture that punishes failure too harshly will therefore tend to suppress not only mistakes but the experiments from which breakthroughs come, because people quickly learn that the safest course is to attempt nothing unusual. Dr Stefan Holt has shown that the most inventive organisations are not those with the cleverest staff but those that make it psychologically safe to propose a strange idea and to be wrong, treating failed attempts as the ordinary cost of discovery rather than as cause for blame. Where failure is shameful, people protect themselves by proposing only what is sure to work, and what is sure to work is rarely new. To fear being wrong, in short, is to forfeit the very chance of being original.

¶6 Time, too, plays a part that is easily overlooked. Many famous insights arrived only after a problem had been set aside, during a walk, a holiday or a night's sleep, when the conscious mind had stopped wrestling with it. This is not idleness but a recognised stage of creative work, in which the mind continues to process a problem below the level of awareness and may suddenly offer up a solution. A schedule packed so tightly that it leaves no room for the mind to wander may, paradoxically, be one of the surest ways to prevent good ideas from forming at all. Rest, on this view, is not the opposite of work but a hidden and necessary part of it. The wandering mind is, surprisingly often, the working mind.

¶7 The implications run counter to much conventional wisdom. If creativity depends on connection, variety, the freedom to fail and unhurried time, then the modern obsession with constant productivity and measurable output may be quietly corrosive of the very originality it claims to want. Schools that drill children for examinations, and workplaces that fill every hour with meetings, may be optimising for the predictable while starving the conditions in which the genuinely new appears. The good idea, it turns out, is rarely the product of trying harder. It is the product of building the kind of life, and the kind of institution, in which ideas can find one another. We cannot order originality on demand, but we can, with some care, prepare the ground in which it tends to grow. That, perhaps, is the most practical lesson the long study of creativity has to offer.

questions.text: The writer accepts the popular image of ideas arriving as a single sudden flash.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says important ideas 'almost never arrive complete in a single stroke', rejecting the flash image.

evidence.quote: almost never arrive complete in a single stroke

questions.text: According to the passage, most new ideas are combinations of existing ones.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says 'most are combinations of older ones', the unexpected joining of existing things.

evidence.quote: most are combinations of older ones

questions.text: Creativity is spread evenly across all places and times.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says 'creativity clusters', with certain places producing a disproportionate share, contradicting even spread.

evidence.quote: creativity clusters

questions.text: Creative clusters arise mainly because more geniuses happen to be born there.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says 'The cause is rarely a sudden concentration of born geniuses', but rather mixing and exchange.

evidence.quote: The cause is rarely a sudden concentration of born geniuses

questions.text: Children today spend more years in school than a century ago.
questions.explanation: Schooling is mentioned in the conclusion, but the passage makes no claim about years spent in school over time, so this is Not Given.

questions.text: The writer thinks an excessive focus on constant productivity can harm creativity.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 says the obsession with constant productivity 'may be quietly corrosive of the very originality it claims to want'.

evidence.quote: may be quietly corrosive of the very originality it claims to want

questions.text: The example of the printing press is used to show that inventions often
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says the press 'combined the wine press with movable type', illustrating combination.

evidence.quote: The printing press combined the wine press with movable type

questions.text: According to the passage, creativity is best understood as a property of
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 concludes creativity is 'less a property of individuals than of the connections between them'.

evidence.quote: less a property of individuals than of the connections between them

questions.text: Why does a culture that punishes failure tend to reduce innovation?
options: people learn that the safest course is to attempt nothing unusual
it has no money for research
it works its staff too hard
correct_answer: people learn that the safest course is to attempt nothing unusual
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says people 'quickly learn that the safest course is to attempt nothing unusual'.

evidence.quote: the safest course is to attempt nothing unusual

questions.text: Many famous insights are said to arrive when a problem has been
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 says insights often arrived 'only after a problem had been set aside', during a walk or sleep.

evidence.quote: only after a problem had been set aside

questions.text: The conditions surrounding a person matter at least as much as their individual talent.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4: Mbeki argues 'the conditions surrounding a person matter at least as much as the talent within them'.

evidence.quote: the conditions surrounding a person matter at least as much as the talent within them

questions.text: The most inventive organisations make it psychologically safe to propose strange ideas and be wrong.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5: Holt has shown inventive organisations 'make it psychologically safe to propose a strange idea and to be wrong'.

evidence.quote: make it psychologically safe to propose a strange idea and to be wrong

questions.text: At first, almost every genuinely new idea is hard to tell apart from a ______ one.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says a new idea is 'at first, indistinguishable from a bad one'.

evidence.quote: indistinguishable from a bad one

questions.text: The stage in which the mind keeps working on a problem below awareness depends on having unhurried ______.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 treats unstructured time as a recognised stage of creative work; Paragraph 7 lists 'unhurried time' as a condition for creativity.

evidence.quote: the freedom to fail and unhurried time
